mnemonics.title: File System Layers Order
mnemonics.content: Please Don't Throw Sales People Away - Physical Device, Device Driver, Basic FS, File Org Module, Logical FS, Application Interface

mnemonics.title: Inode Contents
mnemonics.content: Big Pigs Fly Around Over Dirty Grounds - Block pointers, Permissions, Flags (type), Access times, Owner info, Directory entries link count, Group ID, Size

mnemonics.title: Allocation Methods
mnemonics.content: Contiguous = CONstant location; Linked = LINKs to next; Indexed = INDEX block points to data

acronyms.usage: In-memory and on-disk structure containing file metadata like name, attributes, and location
Unix file system structure that stores file metadata and data block pointers
NTFS equivalent of inode table containing file records
Kernel layer providing unified interface for different file system types
Interface allowing user-space programs to create their own file systems

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a book (file) with a table of contents (index block) - to find any page (block), you first check the table of contents instead of flipping through every page. The index block lists page numbers for all data blocks.

Think of seating in a movie theater - each group (file) needs consecutive seats (blocks). Easy to find all seats together, but if someone leaves a gap between groups, you can't seat a large group even if total empty seats exist (external fragmentation).

Think of a treasure hunt - each clue (block) points to the location of the next clue. You must follow each clue to find all treasures. Fast sequential reading but very slow if you want to jump to a specific clue in the middle.

analogies.analogy: Think of a librarian's desk - instead of going to the stacks (disk) every time someone asks for a book, the librarian keeps frequently requested books on the desk (memory). Serving from the desk is instant, while going to the stacks takes much longer.

Directories are like folders in a filing cabinet. Each folder (directory) contains labels (file names) that tell you where to find documents (files). You navigate from the main cabinet (root) through drawers (subdirectories) to find your documents.

The filename is like a person's name (what we call them), while the inode is like their ID number (unique identifier in the system). Many people can refer to the same person by name (hard links), but there's only one actual person with one ID.
